title: Flatten multilevel DLL
description: Flatten a doubly linked list with child pointers

examples.explanation: Start at head 1. Node 1 has no child, move to 2. Node 2 has no child, move to 3. Node 3 has child 7. Recursively flatten child list starting at 7. In child list: node 7 has no child, move to 8. Node 8 has child 11. Recursively flatten child list starting at 11. In child list: node 11 has no child, move to 12. Node 12 has no child, end of child list. Return head=11, tail=12. Insert 11-12 after 8: link 8.next=11, 11.prev=8, 12.next=9, 9.prev=12. Set 8.child=null. Continue in child list: move to 9, no child, move to 10, no child, end. Return head=7, tail=10. Insert 7-10 after 3: link 3.next=7, 7.prev=3, 10.next=4, 4.prev=10. Set 3.child=null. Continue in main list: move to 4, no child, move to 5, no child, move to 6, no child, end. Final flattened list: 1-2-3-7-8-11-12-9-10-4-5-6.

Start at head 1. Node 1 has child 3. Recursively flatten child list starting at 3. In child list: node 3 has no child, move to 4, no child, end. Return head=3, tail=4. Insert 3-4 after 1: link 1.next=3, 3.prev=1, 4.next=2, 2.prev=4. Set 1.child=null. Continue in main list: move to 2, no child, end. Final flattened list: 1-3-4-2.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive DFS Flatten
approaches.summary: Traverse the list recursively, flattening child sublists by linking them into the main list when a child is encountered. This approach uses recursion to handle nested child levels, making it intuitive for tree-like structures.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(d) where d is the maximum depth of child pointers (due to recursion stack)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive DFS Flatten of Multilevel Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Complete solution to flatten a multilevel doubly linked list using recursive DFS.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to treat the multilevel DLL as a tree-like structure where each node can have a child pointer to another DLL. We traverse the main list sequentially. When we encounter a node with a child, we recursively flatten that child sublist and insert it between the current node and its next node. This ensures all child levels are processed depth-first, maintaining the correct order. The algorithm starts at the head and iterates through nodes. For each node: if it has a child, we recursively flatten the child list, which returns the head and tail of the flattened child sublist. We then adjust pointers: link the current node's next to the child head, the child head's prev to the current node, and the child tail's next to the original next node (if it exists). We also set the child pointer to null. This process continues until the end of the list. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the total number of nodes across all levels, as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(d) where d is the maximum depth of child pointers, due to the recursion stack. This approach is intuitive because it mirrors DFS traversal in trees, making it easy to handle nested structures. Common mistakes include not properly handling edge cases like null pointers or forgetting to update the prev pointers in the doubly linked list. To avoid these, always check for null before accessing node properties and ensure all pointer updates are symmetric. The connection to the parent problem is direct: this is the complete implementation of the recursive DFS flatten method.

examples.trace: Start at head 1. Node 1 has no child, move to 2. Node 2 has no child, move to 3. Node 3 has child 7. Recursively flatten child list starting at 7. In child list: node 7 has no child, move to 8. Node 8 has child 11. Recursively flatten child list starting at 11. In child list: node 11 has no child, move to 12. Node 12 has no child, end of child list. Return head=11, tail=12. Insert 11-12 after 8: link 8.next=11, 11.prev=8, 12.next=9, 9.prev=12. Set 8.child=null. Continue in child list: move to 9, no child, move to 10, no child, end. Return head=7, tail=10. Insert 7-10 after 3: link 3.next=7, 7.prev=3, 10.next=4, 4.prev=10. Set 3.child=null. Continue in main list: move to 4, no child, move to 5, no child, move to 6, no child, end. Final flattened list: 1-2-3-7-8-11-12-9-10-4-5-6.

Start at head 1. Node 1 has child 3. Recursively flatten child list starting at 3. In child list: node 3 has no child, move to 4, no child, end. Return head=3, tail=4. Insert 3-4 after 1: link 1.next=3, 3.prev=1, 4.next=2, 2.prev=4. Set 1.child=null. Continue in main list: move to 2, no child, end. Final flattened list: 1-3-4-2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Flatten Helper Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that flattens a sublist starting from a given node and returns its head and tail.
ladders.explanation: This helper function is the core of the recursive DFS approach. It takes a node as input and recursively flattens the list starting from that node, including all its child sublists. The intuition is to process each node depth-first: for a given node, if it has a child, we recursively flatten the child list, which gives us the head and tail of the flattened child sublist. We then insert this sublist between the current node and its original next node. The function returns the head and tail of the flattened list starting from the input node. This allows the parent call to link the flattened sublist correctly. The algorithm: if the node is null, return (null, null). Initialize current = node, tail = node. While current is not null: if current has a child, recursively call the helper on current.child to get child_head and child_tail. Save current.next as next_node. Link current.next to child_head, child_head.prev to current. If next_node is not null, link child_tail.next to next_node and next_node.prev to child_tail. Set current.child to null. Update tail to child_tail (since we've extended the list). Move current to next_node (or child_tail.next if next_node was null). After the loop, return (node, tail). Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(m) where m is the number of nodes in the sublist, as each node is processed once. Space complexity is O(d) for recursion depth. Common mistakes include not updating the tail correctly when inserting child lists or forgetting to handle null pointers for prev/next links. To avoid, always update tail to the last node processed and check for null before linking. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by being called from the main flatten function to handle child sublists recursively.

examples.trace: Call helper with node 3. Current=3, tail=3. Current has child 7. Recursively call helper on child 7. In recursive call: current=7, tail=7. No child, move to 8: current=8, tail=8. No child, move to 9: current=9, tail=9. No child, end. Return (7,9). Back to main call: save next_node=4. Link 3.next=7, 7.prev=3. Link 9.next=4, 4.prev=9. Set 3.child=null. Update tail=9. Move current=4. Current has no child, update tail=4. End loop. Return (3,9).

Call helper with node 1. Current=1, tail=1. Current has child 2. Recursively call helper on child 2. In recursive call: current=2, tail=2. No child, end. Return (2,2). Back to main call: save next_node=null. Link 1.next=2, 2.prev=1. Since next_node is null, no link from child tail. Set 1.child=null. Update tail=2. Move current=null. End loop. Return (1,2).

testCases.input: Node: 1 with child 2->3 with child 4, next 5
Node: 1 with child 2 with child 3, next 4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inserting a Flattened Child List into Main List
ladders.desc: The technique of linking a flattened child sublist between a node and its next node in the main list.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves inserting a flattened child doubly linked list between a current node and its original next node in the main list. The intuition is to maintain the doubly linked structure while integrating the child list seamlessly. When a node has a child, after recursively flattening the child list, we have the head and tail of that child sublist. We need to insert this sublist so that the current node points to the child head, and the child tail points to the original next node. This requires updating four pointers: current.next, child_head.prev, child_tail.next, and next_node.prev (if next_node exists). The steps: 1. Save the original next node of the current node (next_node = current.next). 2. Link current.next to child_head, and child_head.prev to current. 3. If next_node is not null, link child_tail.next to next_node and next_node.prev to child_tail. 4. Set current.child to null to remove the child pointer. This ensures the child list is now part of the main list, and the order is preserved. Time complexity is O(1) for the insertion operation itself, but it relies on having the child list already flattened. Space complexity is O(1) for pointer adjustments. Common mistakes include forgetting to update the prev pointers (since it's a doubly linked list) or not handling the case when next_node is null. To avoid, always update both next and prev pointers symmetrically and check for null before accessing. This logic is central to L1 and L0 because it's the mechanism that integrates child sublists into the main list during recursion.

examples.trace: Save next_node=4. Link 3.next=7. Link 7.prev=3. Since next_node=4 is not null, link 9.next=4 and 4.prev=9. Set 3.child=null. Result: node 3 now points to 7, 9 points to 4, and prev pointers are updated: 7.prev=3, 4.prev=9.

examples.input: Current node: 1, child_head: 2, child_tail: 2, next_node: null
examples.trace: Save next_node=null. Link 1.next=2. Link 2.prev=1. Since next_node is null, no link from child_tail. Set 1.child=null. Result: node 1 points to 2, 2.prev=1, and 2.next remains as was (null or whatever).

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Doubly Linked List with Child Pointers
ladders.desc: Iterating through a doubly linked list while handling nodes that may have child pointers.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a doubly linked list where each node has next, prev, and child pointers. The intuition is to move sequentially through the list, but when a node has a child, we need to account for the possibility of diving into a sublist. In the context of flattening, we don't immediately process the child during traversal; instead, we use recursion to handle it later. However, understanding how to traverse such a structure is key. The algorithm: start at the head node. While the current node is not null, you can access current.next to move forward, current.prev to move backward (though typically we move forward), and current.child to check for a child sublist. In a simple traversal without flattening, you might just ignore child pointers or treat them as a separate traversal. For flattening, the traversal is interwoven with recursive calls. Time complexity for traversal is O(n) if we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(1) for iterative traversal, but O(d) if recursion is involved. Common mistakes include infinite loops if child pointers form cycles or not updating the current pointer correctly after inserting child lists. To avoid, ensure child pointers are set to null after processing and always move to the correct next node. This block feeds into L2 and above because traversing the list is necessary to identify where to insert child sublists.

examples.input: List: 1<->2<->3 with child at 2 pointing to 4<->5
examples.trace: Start at 1. Node 1: no child, move to 2. Node 2: has child 4. If ignoring child, move to 3. If exploring child, traverse child list: 4, then 5, then return to 2 and move to 3. End.
Start at 1. Node 1: has child 2. If exploring child, traverse child list: 2. End.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Linking Nodes in a Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of setting next and prev pointers between two nodes in a DLL.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves connecting two nodes in a doubly linked list by updating their next and prev pointers. In a DLL, each node has pointers to both its next node and previous node, allowing bidirectional traversal. Linking two nodes, say node A and node B, means setting A.next = B and B.prev = A. This establishes a forward link from A to B and a backward link from B to A. The intuition is that these pointers maintain the list structure; proper linking ensures the list remains connected and can be traversed in both directions. The algorithm is straightforward: given nodes A and B, if A is not null, set A.next = B; if B is not null, set B.prev = A. It's crucial to check for null to avoid errors. Time complexity is O(1), and space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include forgetting to set both pointers (especially prev in a DLL) or linking nodes in the wrong order, which can break the list. To avoid, always update both next and prev symmetrically and verify the direction of linkage. This operation is foundational for L3 and above because inserting child lists or traversing requires linking nodes to maintain the DLL structure.

examples.trace: Check node 1 is not null: set 1.next = 2. Check node 2 is not null: set 2.prev = 1. Result: 1 points forward to 2, and 2 points back to 1.
Check node 3 is not null: set 3.next = null. Since the second node is null, no prev pointer to set. Result: 3 points to null, indicating end of list.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Node Structure in a Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic building block: a node with data, next, and prev pointers.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the node structure used in a doubly linked list (DLL). A node is a fundamental unit that contains data (e.g., an integer value) and two pointers: next and prev. The next pointer points to the following node in the list, and the prev pointer points to the previous node. This allows traversal in both forward and backward directions, unlike a singly linked list which only has a next pointer. The intuition is that each node is connected to its neighbors, forming a chain. In a multilevel DLL, an additional child pointer may exist, pointing to another DLL, but at this level, we focus on the basic next and prev pointers. The node can be defined in pseudocode as a class with attributes: val (data), prev, next, and optionally child. For example, in Python-style: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.prev = None; self.next = None; self.child = None. Time and space complexity are not applicable here as this is a data structure definition. Common mistakes include confusing prev and next pointers or not initializing them to null. To avoid, remember that prev points backward and next points forward, and always set pointers to null when creating a new node. This concept is prerequisite for L4 and above because linking nodes requires understanding their pointer structure.

examples.trace: Initialize a Node object. Set val=1. Set prev=None. Set next=None. Set child=None. Result: a standalone node not linked to any other nodes.

examples.input: Two nodes: node A with val=1, node B with val=2
examples.trace: Create node A: set val=1, prev=None, next=None. Create node B: set val=2, prev=None, next=None. They are independent nodes until linked.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative Stack Flatten
approaches.summary: Use an explicit stack to store next nodes when a child is found, allowing iterative traversal and flattening of the list. This avoids recursion overhead and is more memory-efficient in languages with limited recursion depth.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(d) where d is the maximum depth of child pointers (due to stack storage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Flatten Multilevel Doubly Linked List Using Iterative Stack
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution using a stack to flatten a multilevel doubly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires flattening a multilevel doubly linked list where each node has next, prev, and child pointers. The iterative stack approach processes nodes in depth-first order while maintaining proper doubly linked list connections. The intuition is to traverse the list linearly, but when encountering a child node, we need to process that child's entire sublist before continuing with the original next node. A stack perfectly handles this by storing the next node when we encounter a child, allowing us to return to it later. We start with the head node and maintain a current pointer. At each step: if current has a child, we push current.next onto stack (if it exists), connect current to child, update prev pointer of child, set child to null, and move to child. If current has no child, we check if current.next exists - if yes, we move to next; if no, we pop from stack (if not empty) and connect current to that popped node, updating pointers appropriately. This ensures all child lists are processed before returning to higher-level next nodes. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node exactly once. Space complexity is O(d) where d is maximum depth of child pointers, as stack stores at most one node per depth level. This approach avoids recursion overhead and is suitable for languages with limited recursion depth. Common mistakes include forgetting to update prev pointers when connecting nodes, not setting child pointers to null after processing, and mishandling empty stack cases. The real pattern involves careful pointer manipulation while maintaining stack discipline.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at node 1 (no child, next=2) -> move to 2
Step 2: Node 2 (no child, next=3) -> move to 3
Step 3: Node 3 has child 7 -> push 4 onto stack, connect 3 to 7, set 7.prev=3, set 3.child=null, move to 7
Step 4: Node 7 (no child, next=8) -> move to 8
Step 5: Node 8 has child 11 -> push 9 onto stack, connect 8 to 11, set 11.prev=8, set 8.child=null, move to 11
Step 6: Node 11 (no child, next=12) -> move to 12
Step 7: Node 12 (no child, next=null) -> stack not empty, pop 9, connect 12 to 9, set 9.prev=12, move to 9
Step 8: Node 9 (no child, next=10) -> move to 10
Step 9: Node 10 (no child, next=null) -> stack not empty, pop 4, connect 10 to 4, set 4.prev=10, move to 4
Step 10: Node 4 (no child, next=5) -> move to 5
Step 11: Node 5 (no child, next=6) -> move to 6
Step 12: Node 6 (no child, next=null) -> stack empty, done

Step 1: Start at node 1 has child 3 -> push 2 onto stack, connect 1 to 3, set 3.prev=1, set 1.child=null, move to 3
Step 2: Node 3 (no child, next=4) -> move to 4
Step 3: Node 4 (no child, next=null) -> stack not empty, pop 2, connect 4 to 2, set 2.prev=4, move to 2
Step 4: Node 2 (no child, next=null) -> stack empty, done

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Process Node with Stack in Multilevel List
ladders.desc: The core function that processes a single node, handling child detection and stack operations.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine represents the heart of the iterative stack approach. Given a current node in a multilevel doubly linked list, it determines whether to process a child branch or continue with the next node, using a stack to save next nodes when diving into children. The intuition is that at each node, we must decide: if there's a child, we need to explore that entire sublist immediately, but we must remember where to return afterward. The stack serves as our memory for return points. The algorithm: 1. If current has a child: push current.next onto stack (if not null), set current.next = current.child, set current.child.prev = current, set current.child = null, move current to current.next. 2. Else if current.next exists: move current to current.next. 3. Else (current.next is null): if stack is not empty, pop node from stack, set current.next = popped node, set popped.prev = current, move current to current.next. This ensures depth-first traversal. Time complexity per call is O(1), but overall O(n) across all nodes. Space complexity depends on stack usage. This feeds into the full problem by being the repeated operation in the main traversal loop. Common mistakes include forgetting to update prev pointers when connecting nodes, not checking for null before pushing/popping, and incorrectly handling the case where both child and next exist. The pattern involves careful conditional checks and stack operations.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check node 3 has child 7 -> true
Step 2: Push next node 4 onto stack -> stack now [4]
Step 3: Set 3.next = 7
Step 4: Set 7.prev = 3
Step 5: Set 3.child = null
Step 6: Move current to 7
Result: Current is now 7, stack has [4]

examples.input: Current node: 12 (no child, next null), Stack: [9]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Check node 12 has child -> false
Step 2: Check 12.next -> null
Step 3: Stack not empty, pop 9
Step 4: Set 12.next = 9
Step 5: Set 9.prev = 12
Step 6: Move current to 9
Result: Current is now 9, stack is empty

testCases.expected: Push 9, connect to 11, move to 11, stack [9]
Pop 4, connect to 4, move to 4, stack []
Push 2, connect to 3, move to 3, stack [2]
Push 7, connect to 8, move to 8, stack [10,7]
Pop 12, connect to 12, move to 12, stack [13]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Depth-First Traversal with Stack for Multilevel Structure
ladders.desc: The central technique of using stack to achieve depth-first traversal without recursion.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core algorithmic insight: using an explicit stack to traverse a multilevel structure in depth-first order iteratively. In a multilevel doubly linked list, when we encounter a node with a child, we need to fully traverse the child's sublist before continuing with the node's original next. This is naturally a depth-first pattern. The stack allows us to save the 'next' nodes that we need to return to later. The intuition is similar to how recursion uses the call stack, but here we manage it explicitly. The algorithm maintains a current pointer and a stack. While current is not null: if current has child, push current.next, move to child; else if current.next exists, move to next; else pop from stack and connect. This ensures we always go deep into child chains first, then backtrack using the stack. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is processed once. Space complexity is O(d) for stack depth. This is the key technique that enables the full solution. Common mistakes include pushing nodes incorrectly (e.g., pushing child instead of next), not handling empty stack properly, and infinite loops from incorrect pointer updates. The pattern demonstrates how stacks can simulate recursive traversal.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Current=1 (no child) -> move to 2
Step 2: Current=2 (no child) -> move to 3
Step 3: Current=3 has child 4 -> push 3.next (null), move to 4
Step 4: Current=4 (no child, next=5) -> move to 5
Step 5: Current=5 (no child, next=null) -> stack pop null (ignore), done

examples.input: List: A->B (B has child C->D), B.next=E
examples.trace:
Step 1: Current=A (no child) -> move to B
Step 2: Current=B has child C -> push E, move to C
Step 3: Current=C (no child, next=D) -> move to D
Step 4: Current=D (no child, next=null) -> stack pop E, connect D to E, move to E
Step 5: Current=E (no child, next=null) -> stack empty, done

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Saving and Restoring Next Pointers
ladders.desc: How to use a stack to save next nodes and restore them later during traversal.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the stack mechanics essential for the approach. A stack (LIFO - Last In First Out) is used to store nodes that represent where to continue after processing a child chain. When we encounter a node with a child, we push its next node onto the stack. Later, when we reach the end of a chain (current.next is null), we pop from the stack to get the saved next node and connect it. The intuition is that the stack preserves the order of 'next' nodes we need to return to, with the most recently saved node being the one we need next (depth-first). The algorithm involves: push(node) when node has child and node.next exists, pop() when current.next is null and stack not empty. We must also handle the connections: when popping, set current.next = popped_node and popped_node.prev = current. Time complexity for push/pop is O(1). Space complexity depends on maximum stack depth. This building block enables the core logic by providing the memory mechanism. Common mistakes include pushing null nodes (should only push non-null), not checking stack emptiness before pop, and incorrect pointer updates after popping. The pattern shows how stacks can manage pending tasks in traversal.

examples.input: Push scenario: At node 3 with next=4, child exists
examples.trace:
Step 1: Node 3 has child, and 3.next is 4 (not null)
Step 2: Push 4 onto stack
Step 3: Stack now contains node 4
Note: If 3.next were null, we would not push anything

examples.input: Pop scenario: Current at node 12 with next=null, stack has [9]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Current node 12 has next=null
Step 2: Stack is not empty (has [9])
Step 3: Pop 9 from stack
Step 4: Set 12.next = 9
Step 5: Set 9.prev = 12
Step 6: Stack is now empty

testCases.input: Pop from stack with one element
Push after pop: stack [X], pop, then push Y
testCases.expected: After pop stack [], after push stack [Y]

testCases.input: Interleaved: push A, pop, push B
testCases.expected: After push A: [A], after pop: [], after push B: [B]

testCases.input: Push same node twice (if scenario allows)
testCases.expected: Stack contains two references to same node

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Doubly Linked List Pointer Manipulation
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations for updating next and prev pointers in a doubly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic pointer manipulations required for the flattening algorithm. In a doubly linked list, each node has next (pointer to following node) and prev (pointer to previous node). When flattening a multilevel list, we need to connect nodes by updating these pointers. Key operations: 1. Setting next pointer: nodeA.next = nodeB. 2. Setting prev pointer: nodeB.prev = nodeA. 3. Checking for null pointers before operations. The intuition is that we must maintain the doubly linked invariant: if A.next = B, then B.prev should equal A (unless B is null). When we insert a child list between two nodes, we update four pointers: current.next to child, child.prev to current, last node of child chain to saved next, and saved next.prev to last node. Time complexity per operation is O(1). This basic operation is essential for all higher levels. Common mistakes include forgetting to update prev pointers (leading to broken links), dereferencing null pointers, and not handling edge cases (like empty lists). The pattern involves careful assignment and null checks.

examples.input: Connect node 3 to node 7 (where 3.next was 4, 7.prev was null)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Store 3.next (which is 4) for later use
Step 2: Set 3.next = 7
Step 3: Set 7.prev = 3
Step 4: Now 3 and 7 are directly connected
Note: The connection from 7 to its original next (if any) remains

examples.input: Connect node 12 to node 9 (where 12.next was null, 9.prev was null)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Set 12.next = 9
Step 2: Set 9.prev = 12
Step 3: Now 12 and 9 are connected
Note: Both had null pointers before, so this establishes new links

testCases.input: Connect A to B and B to A (both directions)
Update existing link: change A.next from B to C
testCases.expected: A.next=C, need to update B.prev if necessary

testCases.input: Handle null: set A.next = B when B is null

testCases.expected: Chain A->B->C with proper prev links
Must be done as separate assignments

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Doubly Linked Lists and Stack Data Structures
ladders.desc: Prerequisite knowledge of doubly linked lists and stacks for beginners.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the two key data structures needed for the problem. A doubly linked list is a linear data structure where each node contains data and two pointers: next (points to the following node) and prev (points to the previous node). The first node's prev is null, and the last node's next is null. Traversal can be done forward using next or backward using prev. A stack is a LIFO (Last In First Out) data structure with two main operations: push (adds an element to the top) and pop (removes and returns the top element). It can be implemented using arrays or linked lists. The intuition for using them together: the doubly linked list represents our data with multilevel connections, and the stack helps us manage the order of processing. For example, if we have a list A->B->C and B has a child X->Y, we need to visit X and Y before C. A stack lets us save C while we process X and Y. This is the simplest concept that underpins the entire approach. Common beginner mistakes include confusing next/prev pointers, not initializing pointers to null, and misunderstanding stack order. Real pattern: think of the stack as a 'to-do list' of nodes to visit later.

examples.input: Doubly linked list: Node1 (data=1, next=Node2, prev=null), Node2 (data=2, next=null, prev=Node1)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Node1 has next pointing to Node2, prev is null (it's the head)
Step 2: Node2 has prev pointing to Node1, next is null (it's the tail)
Step 3: From Node1, follow next to Node2
Step 4: From Node2, follow prev back to Node1

examples.input: Stack operations: start empty, push 5, push 3, pop, push 7
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initial stack: []
Step 2: Push 5: stack becomes [5]
Step 3: Push 3: stack becomes [5,3] (3 is top)
Step 4: Pop: removes and returns 3, stack becomes [5]
Step 5: Push 7: stack becomes [5,7] (7 is top)

Add a second node with value 20 after first node
Traverse doubly linked list forward from head

Stack push order: A, B, C
Stack pop order after pushes A,B,C

Check if doubly linked list node is tail

approaches.name: Approach 3: In-Place Flatten with Tail Pointer
approaches.summary: Traverse the list while maintaining a tail pointer to the last node, immediately linking child sublists into the main list as they are encountered. This method flattens the list in a single pass without extra data structures.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) (excluding recursion stack if used, but can be implemented iteratively)

ladders.title: Full Problem — In-Place Flatten with Tail Pointer
ladders.desc: Complete solution to flatten a multilevel doubly linked list using tail pointer traversal
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by traversing the original list once while maintaining a tail pointer that always points to the last node of the flattened portion. When we encounter a node with a child, we immediately flatten that child sublist by linking it into the main list at the current position. The key insight is that we can process child sublists recursively or iteratively as we encounter them, always updating the tail pointer to the end of whatever we've flattened so far. This works because the tail pointer gives us immediate access to where we should attach any child sublist we find. We start with the head node and tail pointer both pointing to the first node. As we traverse, if current node has no child, we simply move forward. If it has a child, we save the next node (if any), then recursively flatten the child sublist starting from the child node. Once the child sublist is flattened, we link it into the main list: current node's next becomes the child, child's prev becomes current node, and we update the tail pointer to the end of the child sublist. Finally, we connect the saved next node (if any) to the end of the child sublist. This continues until we reach the end of the list. The time complexity is O(n) where n is the total number of nodes in the final flattened list, as each node is visited exactly once. Space complexity is O(1) for the iterative version, though a recursive implementation would use O(d) stack space where d is the maximum depth of nesting. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the key subroutine of flattening a child sublist and updating connections properly. Common mistakes include forgetting to update the prev pointers in the child sublist, not properly handling the case where current node has no next after flattening a child, and infinite recursion when child pointers form cycles. The real code pattern involves while loop traversal with conditional child processing and careful pointer updates.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with head=1, tail=1. Current=1, no child, move to 2.
Step 2: Current=2, no child, move to 3.
Step 3: Current=3 has child 7. Save next=4. Flatten child sublist starting at 7.
Step 4: In child sublist: Process 7 (no child), 8 (has child 11). Save next=9. Flatten sub-sublist starting at 11.
Step 5: In sub-sublist: Process 11 (no child), 12 (no child). Return tail=12.
Step 6: Back to 8's sublist: Connect 8->11, 11->prev=8, tail=12. Connect 12->9, 9->prev=12. Process 9 (no child), 10 (no child). Return tail=10.
Step 7: Back to main: Connect 3->7, 7->prev=3, tail=10. Connect 10->4, 4->prev=10.
Step 8: Continue: Current=4 (no child), 5 (no child), 6 (no child). Done.
Final: 1-2-3-7-8-11-12-9-10-4-5-6

Step 1: Start head=1, tail=1. Current=1 has child 3. Save next=2.
Step 2: Flatten child: Process 3 (no child), 4 (no child). Return tail=4.
Step 3: Connect 1->3, 3->prev=1, tail=4. Connect 4->2, 2->prev=4.
Step 4: Current=2 (no child). Done.
Final: 1-3-4-2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Flatten Child Sublist and Update Connections
ladders.desc: The core function that flattens a child sublist and connects it to the main list
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the approach. When we encounter a node with a child pointer, we need to: 1) Save the current node's next pointer (if it exists), 2) Recursively flatten the child sublist starting from the child node, 3) Connect the current node to the flattened child sublist, and 4) Connect the end of the child sublist to the saved next node. The intuition is that we're inserting the entire child sublist between the current node and its original next node. We need to update four pointers: current.next, child.prev, tail_of_child.next, and saved_next.prev. The algorithm: function flattenChild(current, child): save_next = current.next; flattened_tail = recursivelyFlatten(child); current.next = child; child.prev = current; if save_next exists: flattened_tail.next = save_next; save_next.prev = flattened_tail. The time complexity for this subroutine is O(m) where m is the number of nodes in the child sublist, as it processes that entire sublist. Space complexity depends on implementation: O(d) for recursive (depth of nesting) or O(1) for iterative. This feeds into L0 as the critical operation performed whenever we find a node with a child. Common mistakes include forgetting to set child.prev = current, not handling the case where save_next is null, and not properly returning the tail of the flattened child sublist. The real code pattern involves careful pointer manipulation with null checks.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Save save_next = 4
Step 2: Flatten child sublist (already flat), flattened_tail = 9
Step 3: Update pointers: 3.next = 7, 7.prev = 3
Step 4: Connect end: 9.next = 4, 4.prev = 9
Result: 3 connected to 7, 9 connected to 4

Step 1: Save save_next = 9
Step 2: Flatten child sublist, flattened_tail = 12
Step 3: Update: 8.next = 11, 11.prev = 8
Step 4: Connect: 12.next = 9, 9.prev = 12
Result: 8 connected to 11, 12 connected to 9

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tail Pointer Maintenance During Traversal
ladders.desc: Maintaining a tail pointer that always points to the last node of the flattened portion
ladders.explanation: The central technique of this approach is maintaining a tail pointer that always references the last node in the currently flattened list. As we traverse the original list, we update this tail pointer whenever we add new nodes (either by moving to the next node or by flattening a child sublist). The intuition is that the tail pointer gives us immediate O(1) access to where we should attach any child sublist we encounter. Without it, we would need to traverse to the end of the current flattened portion each time, making the algorithm O(n²). The algorithm: Initialize tail = head. While current node exists: If current has child: Process child sublist (which returns its tail). Update connections between current, child sublist, and current.next. Update tail to child sublist's tail. Else: Update tail to current. Move to next node. The key insight is that tail always points to the last node that has been properly connected in the flattened list. Time complexity for this logic is O(n) as we process each node once. Space is O(1). This feeds into L1 as the mechanism that enables efficient child sublist insertion. Common mistakes include not updating tail after flattening a child, updating tail to the wrong node, and forgetting to move tail when there's no child. The real code pattern involves tail variable updated in both branches of the child check.

examples.trace:
Step 1: tail=1, current=1 (no child), tail=1, move to 2
Step 2: tail=2, current=2 (no child), tail=2, move to 3
Step 3: tail=3, current=3 (no child), tail=3, done

Step 1: tail=1, current=1 has child 2
Step 2: Flatten child sublist (2), get tail=2
Step 3: Update connections: 1->2, 2->3
Step 4: Update tail to 2 (end of child sublist)
Step 5: Current moves to 3, tail=3, done

testCases.input: Start: tail at 1, List: 1<->2<->3, No children
Start: tail at 1, List: 1<->2<->3<->4, No children

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing and Processing a Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Basic traversal of a DLL while examining and potentially modifying nodes
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a doubly linked list from head to tail, examining each node's properties (value, next, prev, child), and potentially modifying pointers. The intuition is that we need to visit each node exactly once to check if it has a child that needs processing. Unlike array traversal, linked list traversal requires following pointer chains. The algorithm: current = head; while current != null: Process current node; current = current.next. Processing might involve checking if current.child exists, and if so, performing some operation. We must be careful to save next pointers before modifying them, as once we change current.next, we lose access to the original next node. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n nodes. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 as the foundation for the traversal that uses the tail pointer. Common mistakes include infinite loops from circular references, losing the next pointer after modification, and not handling null pointers properly. The real code pattern is a while loop with a current pointer that moves forward.

examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, process, move to 2
Step 2: current=2, process, move to 3
Step 3: current=3, process, move to 4
Step 4: current=4, process, move to null (end)

Step 1: current=1, detect child exists (value 2), process, move to 3
Step 2: current=3, no child, process, move to null

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pointer Manipulation in Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of updating next and prev pointers in a DLL node
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves modifying the connections between nodes in a doubly linked list. Each node has three pointers: prev (to previous node), next (to next node), and child (to child sublist head). Basic operations include: 1) Linking node A to node B: A.next = B; B.prev = A. 2) Inserting node C between A and B: Save A.next; A.next = C; C.prev = A; C.next = B; B.prev = C. 3) Removing a node. The intuition is that in a DLL, every connection is bidirectional, so we must always update both directions. Forgetting to update prev pointers is a common error. The algorithm for inserting a sublist between two nodes: function insertSublist(before, sublistHead, sublistTail, after): before.next = sublistHead; sublistHead.prev = before; sublistTail.next = after; if after exists: after.prev = sublistTail. Time complexity is O(1) for constant pointer updates. Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as the basic operation performed during traversal when we find a child. Common mistakes include null pointer exceptions, circular references, and updating pointers in wrong order. The real code pattern involves multiple assignment statements with null checks.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Save after = 1.next (which is 2)
Step 2: 1.next = 3
Step 3: 3.prev = 1
Step 4: 4.next = 2
Step 5: 2.prev = 4

Step 1: 5.next = 6
Step 2: 6.prev = 5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Doubly Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic building block: a node with prev, next, and child pointers
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a node in this multilevel doubly linked list looks like. Each node has four fields: 1) val (integer value), 2) prev (pointer to previous node), 3) next (pointer to next node), 4) child (pointer to head of a child sublist, or null). The intuition is that this structure allows hierarchical data: the next pointer creates the main list, while child pointers create sublists that can be flattened. A null pointer indicates the end of a list or absence of a child. The algorithm for creating a node: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.prev = None; self.next = None; self.child = None. Time/space for creating one node is O(1). This feeds into L4 as the basic unit we manipulate with pointer operations. Common mistakes include confusing prev/next directions, forgetting to initialize pointers to null, and not understanding that child can point to another DLL. The real code pattern is a simple class definition.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Allocate memory for node
Step 2: Set val=1
Step 3: Set prev=null
Step 4: Set next=null
Step 5: Set child=null

Step 1: Create Node1 with val=1
Step 2: Create Node2 with val=2
Step 3: Set Node1.next = Node2
Step 4: Set Node2.prev = Node1
